_pass: Level 3 Pass 1 + Rounds 1-3 visual polish + worked examples v2 + LA trim + apps + extra exercises + exercises unify v2
title: Mortgages: 30 years, dollar by dollar
deck: The longest, largest amortizing loan most people ever sign. Each monthly payment splits between interest and principal, and that split shifts month by month across 360 months.
objectives: Analyze a mortgage payment as a split between interest and principal, explain how that split shifts over the life of the loan, and identify the major components of total homeownership cost beyond the loan itself.

content.text: Imagine signing a thirty-year mortgage on a $300,000 house at 7%, with a fixed monthly payment of $1,995.91. The first payment is due on the first of the following month. We send the check, watch the bank deposit it, and pull up the new statement two weeks later. The remaining balance has dropped to $299,754.09. That is the entire month's contribution to paying off the house: a reduction of less than two hundred and fifty dollars on a balance of three hundred thousand.

That is not a mistake or a hidden fee. It is amortization, with the interest piled to the front of the loan. The first payment on a mortgage is overwhelmingly interest; only a sliver of it pays down principal. The split shifts gradually over the life of the loan, until the final few payments are nearly all principal. This section closes the chapter with the longest, biggest amortizing loan most people will ever sign for, and explains why early payments feel like they barely move the needle.
4.6.1  Two lines, every month, for 360 months

Every mortgage payment splits into two precisely defined parts. The interest portion is the current outstanding balance times the monthly periodic rate r/12. The principal portion is whatever is left of the payment M after the interest is taken out. The principal portion reduces the balance for next month, and the cycle repeats. Because the balance is largest in month one, that is when the interest portion is largest and the principal portion is smallest. As the balance shrinks, the interest portion shrinks with it and the principal portion grows.

    <text x="310" y="196" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#95929F" letter-spacing="2">PER-PAYMENT SPLIT, IN ARITHMETIC</text>
    <g font-family="JetBrains Mono, monospace" font-size="12" fill="#F4F2F8">
        <text x="60" y="222">interest = balance × (r/12)</text>
        <text x="60" y="244">         = 300,000 × (0.07/12)</text>

    </g>
    <g font-family="JetBrains Mono, monospace" font-size="12" fill="#F4F2F8">
        <text x="320" y="222">principal = M − interest</text>
        <text x="320" y="244">          = 1,995.91 − 1,750.00</text>
        <text x="320" y="266">          = $245.91</text>
    </g>
    <text x="310" y="290" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Inter, sans-serif" font-size="11" fill="#FF6B8A">→ new balance: 300,000 − 245.91 = $299,754.09</text>
</svg>
content.caption: Figure 4.6. Month one of a $300,000 mortgage at 7% over 30 years. The fixed monthly payment of $1,995.91 splits into $1,750 of interest and only $245.91 of principal, dropping the balance by less than a tenth of a percent.

content.text:
Definition 4.6. An amortization schedule is a month-by-month table showing every payment of an amortizing loan, with each row containing the interest portion, the principal portion, and the remaining balance after the payment. For any given month with starting balance B and fixed payment M:
interest portion = B · [r] / [12],; principal portion = M − interest portion
new balance = B − principal portion

Margin noteAn amortization schedule for a 30-year mortgage has 360 rows. Banks generate the schedule once at closing and provide it to the borrower; tax-time accountants reuse it to compute mortgage-interest deductions. The same table can be built in Excel with two formulas and a drag.

In month one of a 30-year mortgage at 7%, the interest portion is approximately 87.7% of the total payment, and only 12.3% reduces principal. The crossover point, the month when principal first exceeds interest, comes more than two-thirds of the way through the loan, around month 250. The reason is mechanical: interest in any month is computed on the current balance, and the balance is largest at the beginning. The first payment is paying interest on essentially the full principal.
In a 30-year mortgage, the first payment is 88% interest. The last payment is almost all principal. The crossover point lives 250 months in.

Common mistakeAssuming that each payment reduces the balance by the same amount. The monthly payment M is constant, but the split between interest and principal shifts month by month. Reading the first payment as if it were the average payment leads to dramatically wrong predictions about how the balance behaves.

Example 4.6. Month one of a $300K mortgage at 7%, 30 years.

A homeowner closes on a $300,000 mortgage at 7% annual interest, fixed monthly payments over a 30-year term. We want the monthly payment, the split of the first payment into interest and principal, and the new balance after that single payment.

Solution.

Step 1. Compute the monthly payment. With P = 300,000, r/12 ≈ 0.005833, and 12t = 360:

M = 300,000 · [0.005833] / [1 − (1.005833)-360] ≈ $1,995.91

Step 2. Split the first payment into interest and principal.

interest portion = 300,000 × 0.005833 = $1,750.00
principal portion = 1,995.91 − 1,750.00 = $245.91

Step 3. Compute the new balance.

new balance = 300,000 − 245.91 = $299,754.09

The homeowner sends $1,995.91 to the bank, watches the balance drop by $245.91, and notices that $1,750 of the payment went to interest. Over the full life of the loan, the total of all 360 payments is 1,995.91 × 360 ≈ $718,528, of which approximately 718,528 − 300,000 = $418,528 is interest, more than the original price of the house.
4.6.3  Beyond the loan: the rest of homeownership

The monthly mortgage payment is only one slice of the true monthly cost of owning a home. Property taxes, homeowner's insurance, mortgage insurance (for low-down-payment loans), HOA fees, utilities, and routine maintenance all sit on top of the loan. Lenders bundle the first three into the monthly payment via an escrow account, which is why the actual amount charged each month is often labeled PITI: principal, interest, taxes, insurance. A homeowner planning a budget should always think in PITI dollars, not in raw amortization dollars.

In the wildTax law in many countries (the United States included) allows homeowners to deduct mortgage interest from taxable income, which is one reason the early-payment interest pile-up is more bearable than it looks. The same interest, however, is also money that does not build equity in the home. The trade-off between renting and buying is sensitive to exactly these calculations: how much of each monthly payment is going to principal, how much is going to interest, and how those numbers compare to the rent the homeowner would otherwise pay.

content.problem: A $200,000 mortgage at 6% per year has a monthly payment of $1,199.10 on a 30-year term. What is the interest portion of the first payment?
content.answer: $1,000. The monthly rate is 0.06/12 = 0.005, and the interest on the first payment is 200,000 × 0.005 = 1,000.

content.problem: For the loan in the previous problem, what is the principal portion of the first payment, and the new balance after that payment?
For the same loan, what is the total amount paid over the life of the mortgage, and the total interest paid?
content.answer: Total paid: 1,199.10 × 360 ≈ 431,676. Total interest: 431,676 − 200,000 ≈ 231,676. The mortgage borrower pays the bank more in interest than the original price of the home.

content.problem:
Why is the interest portion of a mortgage payment so much larger early in the loan than late?

  1. Banks charge higher interest in the early years by contract.
  2. The outstanding balance is largest in the early months, so the interest on that balance is largest.
  3. Mortgage rates rise automatically over time.
  4. The amortization formula has a bug.
content.answer: B. Interest each month is balance × r/12; the balance is largest at the start of the loan, so the interest portion is largest there too.

content.problem:
What does the acronym PITI stand for in the context of monthly homeownership costs?

  1. Principal, Interest, Taxes, Insurance
  2. Principal, Income, Taxes, Investment
  3. Property, Interest, Time, Insurance
  4. Periodic Interest Tax Index
content.answer: A. PITI bundles the loan-related amounts (principal and interest) with property taxes and homeowner's insurance, the four pieces typically collected together via an escrow account.

content.problem:
An amortization schedule has 360 rows for a 30-year mortgage. In which range of months does the principal portion of the payment first exceed the interest portion?

  1. Within the first 12 months
  2. Around month 100
  3. Around month 250
  4. Only in the final 12 months
content.answer: C. For a 30-year mortgage at typical rates, the crossover lands roughly two-thirds of the way through the term, around month 250.

content.problem: Compute the monthly payment on a **$280,000 mortgage at 6.75% APR for 30 years**. Round to nearest dollar.
Same mortgage as Example 4.6.1. Compute the **total cost** over 30 years and the **total interest** paid.

content.title: Computing the monthly mortgage payment
content.problem: A **$300,000** mortgage at **6.5% APR** for **30 years**. Compute the monthly principal-and-interest payment.
content.solution:
**Apply M = P(r/12) / (1 − (1 + r/12)^(−12t)).**

P=300,000, r=0.065, t=30; so r/12 ≈ 0.005417, 12t = 360.

Numerator: 300,000 × 0.005417 ≈ 1,625.

Denominator: 1 − (1.005417)^(−360) ≈ 1 − 0.1429 = 0.8571.

M ≈ 1,625 / 0.8571 ≈ **$1,896.20/month**.

*Don't forget the rest.* The actual monthly housing cost includes property taxes, homeowners insurance, and (sometimes) private mortgage insurance — often quoted as a **PITI** number. The principal-and-interest M is just one of the four pieces.

content.title: First payment vs last payment — the split flips
content.problem: For the $300,000 / 6.5% / 30-year mortgage from Example 4.6.1, compute the **interest portion** and **principal portion** of the **first** monthly payment, and contrast that with the **last** payment.
content.solution:
**First payment (balance = $300,000).**

Interest portion: 300,000 × (0.065/12) = 300,000 × 0.005417 ≈ **$1,625** of the $1,896.20 payment.

Principal portion: 1,896.20 − 1,625 = **$271.20** — only about 14% of the payment.

**Last payment (balance ≈ $1,886, the remaining principal).**

Interest portion: about 1,886 × 0.005417 ≈ **$10**.

Principal portion: roughly **$1,886** — essentially the whole payment.

*The takeaway.* For the first decade or so of a 30-year mortgage, the lion's share of every payment goes to interest, not principal. This is why early extra principal payments are so impactful: they bypass the interest accrued on that principal for the *remaining 29 years*.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Mortgages are the largest and longest amortizing loans most people will ever hold. The 30-year fixed-rate is the American default. The principal-vs-interest split of each payment shifts over time: early payments are almost all interest; late payments are almost all principal. Total interest on a 30-year mortgage commonly exceeds the loan amount itself.

content.title: The 30-year mortgage in the 2020s housing market
content.content: Median US home price in 2024: **about $420,000**. Median 30-year fixed mortgage rate: **roughly 7%**. Applied: a $336,000 loan (20% down) at 7% for 30 years means a monthly payment of **about $2,235** — not counting property tax, homeowner's insurance, and HOA fees. Over the 30 years, total interest paid: **about $469,000**. That is more than the original loan. Welcome to the central math of American adulthood. Knowing this calculation is what separates a buyer who feels priced out by the headline price from a buyer who can think clearly about the total commitment.

content.text: Six sections ago, the word “loan” was a single term that covered an enormous range of products.
